title: practical english usage - Part 76
content:
Selling insurance is a boring job. (More natural than To sell insurance ...) instead of an infinitive (clause) (►   94).

We   can also use an -ing structure at the beginning of a sentence as the subject, It made him  angry to wait for people who were late.

It's important to practise regularly.

We   more often use a structure with it as a preparatory subject (►  268).

In modern English, this is unusual, and especially unusual in an informal style.

To wait for people who were late made him  angry.

To practise regularly is important.

of a sentence.

An infinitive (or a clause beginning with an infinitive) can be the subject 1  subject:     To   practise         is  important.           / It's  important           to   practise.

92 infinitive       as  subject      or  complement_______ The only thing we can do is (to) accept.

What a fire-door does is (to) delay the spread of a fire.

All I did was (to) give him  a little push.

without to.

Expressions like All I did was, What I do is, etc can be followed by an infinitive 5 after    do Rather than wait any more, I decided to go home by taxi.

Rather than is usually followed by an infinitive without to.

Why don't you do something useful like clean the kitchen?

I have to feed the animals as well as look after the children.

It's as easy to smile as frown.

It's easier to do it yourself than explain to somebody else how to do it.

I'm ready to do anything but work on a farm.

We had nothing to do except look at the sea.

Do you want to have lunch now or wait till later?

I'd like to lie down and sleep.

the second is often without to.

When two infinitive structures are joined by and, or, except, but, than, as or like, 4 after    and,     or,   except,       but,    than,     as   and   like You're looking tired. Why not take a break?

Why stand up if you can sit down? Why sit down if you can lie down?

Why pay more at other shops? We have the lowest prices.

without to. For more details, ►    630.

We   can introduce questions and suggestions with why (not) + infinitive 3 after    why     (not) -ing                                 Section 8 Infinitives,        forms     and   Past   Participles grammar •  93   -ing forms: introduction For spelling of -ing forms, ►    3 46-347.

a good reason to use one of the other terms.

In Practical English Usage the expression '-ing form' is used except when there is English Language, by Quirk, Greenbaum, Leech  and Svartvik (Longman,  1985).

discussion of this point, see Section 17.54 of A  Comprehensive Grammar of the grammarians prefer to avoid the terms 'participle' and 'gerund'. For a detailed can sometimes be difficult to decide which  term to use. For this reason, some The  distinction between 'participles’  and 'gerunds' is not always clear-cut, and it 3 'participles'       and   'gerunds':    an   unclear      difference She's angry about not having been invited. (negative perfect passive) Not knowing what to do, I went home. (negative) She loves being looked at. (passive) Having slept for twelve hours, I felt marvellous. (perfect) Note the structure of perfect, passive and negative -ing forms.

2  perfect,     passive      and   negative       -/ng  forms working men and women (participle) - working conditions (gerund) a sleeping child [sleeping is a participle) - a sleeping pill (sleeping is a gerund) like an adjective. Compare an early train.) a waiting train (= a train that is waiting.  Waiting is a participle, used rather noun. Compare a guest room.) - a waiting room (= a room for waiting.  Waiting is a gerund, used rather like a meaning. Compare: (participles) can be used. The  two structures do not have quite the same kind of Before nouns, both noun-like -ing forms (gerunds) and adjective-like -ing forms called 'gerunds’ .

the past, present or future.) When  they are used more like nouns, they are often participles'. (This is not a very suitable name, because these forms can refer to When -ing forms are used verbally or adjectivally, they are often called 'present Smoking is bad for you. (noun-like: subject of sentence) of cigarette end) There was a smoking cigarette end in the ashtray. (adjective-like: description You're smoking too much  these days. (verb: part of present progressive) adjectives or nouns. Compare: We   can use -ing forms (e.g. smoking, walking) not only as verbs, but also like 1  'participles       and   'gerunds' 93 -ing    forms:    intr  oducti    on  ________________ For structures like He made it difficult to refuse, ►    269.
